result:
## The Hum of the Refrigerator

First, the sound. The low, steady hum of the refrigerator in the next room. A sound so constant it paints the silence around it. I’m aware of it now, because you asked me to be aware—to write whatever comes. It’s a grounding wire. That hum means preservation, a slow, electrical fight against entropy. Inside, the lettuce is wilting anyway. It always does.

A train whistle, Doppler-shifting in the distance. Someone is going somewhere, or something is being brought. The nineteenth century still haunting the twenty-first with its lonesome, iron song. It makes me think of my grandfather’s hands, grease under the nails, permanent from a lifetime of maintaining the very machines that passed in the night. He never rode one, not for pleasure. He just kept them rolling for others.

The mind is a desperate magpie. It picks up the shiny and the sorrowful and tries to make a nest. The feel of a cool porcelain mug. The exact, acidic smell of a paperback book opened after twenty years in a box. The emergency of a memory: being eight, and skidding my bike on gravel, the shocking pink of raw flesh on my palm, and the profound, theatrical certainty that this was it, the end. The scar is still there, a tiny, glossy moon on the heel of my hand. A monument to a forgotten catastrophe.

Out the window, a maple tree is staging its autumn rebellion. A blaze of orange against the stern gray of the siding. It’s so beautiful it feels like an argument. *See?* it says. *Letting go can be spectacular.* But I know the next act is bare branches and a carpet of brown, sodden leaves. The cycle isn’t comforting; it’s just relentless. We call it poetry to make the relentlessness bearable.

I think of the 1000 words. Not as a limit, but as a volume, a block of psychic material to be carved. I’ve used some. There are some left. This is the consciousness of creation—the awareness of the chunk of time or stone you’ve been given. Most of life is spent unaware of the word count, the breath count, the heartbeat tally. We just talk, breathe, live, until the abrupt white space at the end.

What do we owe each other? The question surfaces, unbidden. We pass in the grocery aisle, eyes sliding away from the intimate contents of strangers’ carts. We offer a “fine, thanks” to a cashier’s “how are you?” It’s a contract of benign neglect. But sometimes, a crack appears. The barista with tired eyes who hands you your coffee and says, “Have a good day,” and you hear it—the tiny fracture in the script. And you look up and say, “I hope your shift goes quickly,” and for a second, you are both real to each other. It’s exhausting to do that always. It’s unbearable to never do it.

The refrigerator cycles off. The sudden quiet is louder than the hum. In the vacuum, I hear my own heart, or maybe it’s the blood in my ears. A reminder: you are a machine, too. A soft, wet machine, dreaming of meaning. You require maintenance. You are fighting entropy, for a while.

There is a planet, spinning in a distant system, where the sky is a perpetual lavender twilight, and the rain smells of cinnamon. No one will ever know. There are forgotten languages whose last word for “home” or “longing” has been erased from the universe. We are building new ones. “Algorithm.” “Doomscroll.” “Substack.” Each a tiny clay shard of our current dig.

I’m thinking about love, of course. Because when you try to write “whatever comes,” it always circles back to love, or the lack of it. Not just the grand, dramatic kinds. The specific grammar of a long marriage: the silent agreement over who gets the weird side of the pillow, the shared dictionary of looks across a crowded room, the forgiveness of small, daily failures like leaving dishes by the sink. It’s the mortar, not the bricks. It’s dreadfully unsexy and it’s the only thing that holds the wall up.

And art. The desperate, glorious attempt to push a piece of your consciousness through the keyhole of someone else’s skull. A song, a painting, a line of code, a perfectly planted garden. This note in a bottle: *I was here. I felt this. The hum and the train and the tree and the scar. Does it map onto your territory?* The hope is not for a reply, but for the quiet, resonant *click* of recognition. A silent “me too.”

The hum starts again. Faithful. The fight resumes.

I am afraid of the big things—the heating planet, the fraying civility, the silent, chilling technologies. But just now, I am also afraid of small things: that I have already had the best conversation of my life and didn’t know it; that I’ve already read the book that will change me most; that the squirrel on the fence is just living, not posing as a metaphor for my distraction.

We are stories we tell ourselves, with terrible gaps in the narrative. We connect the dots of random events and call it a plot. We search for foreshadowing in the tea leaves of the past.

Maybe that’s the task. To be the scribe of your own hum. To note the quality of the light on the plaster wall (right now, pale yellow, diffuse). To acknowledge the bitter tang of regret for something you didn’t even choose. To feel the immense, ridiculous privilege of being a collection of atoms that can wonder about its own collection.

The word count is dwindling. A constraint creates the form. The dying tree is most itself when it’s letting go. The train’s purpose is in moving, not in arriving.

So here, in this last handful of words, is the only truth I can fish from the stream: Pay attention. To the hum. To the crack in the voice. To the scarlet leaf clinging to the gray branch. It’s all a message, and you are both the sender and the receiver. The universe is whispering to itself in the dark. You are how it hears.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
